content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
1 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gregory G. Diaz 
City Attorney 
City of San Buenaventura 
501 Poli Street 
P. 0. Box 99 
Ventura, California 93002-0099 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-141 
Dear Mr. Diaz: 
July 5, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby 
( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
May Ventura Deputy Mayor Neal Andrews make, participate in making, or influence 
decisions concerning the City's adoption of a Climate Action Plan? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Decisions concerning the Climate Action Plan do not appear to have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on Deputy Mayor Andrews' investment interests. 
FACTS 
California has adopted statewide legislation addressing various aspects of climate change 
and GHG emissions mitigation. Much of this legislation is not directed at citizens or jurisdictions 
specifically, but rather establishes a broad framework for the State's long-term GHG reduction and 
climate change adaptation program. Assembly Bill (AB 32) codified the State's greenhouse gas 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-141 
Page No. 2 
( .. GHG") emissions target by requiring that the State's GHG emissions be reduced to 1990 levels by 
2020. 
A Climate Action Plan is a comprehensive roadmap that outlines specific activities a 
municipality can undertake to reduce GHG emissions locally. Ideally, it also includes an 
implementation strategy that identifies required resources and funding mechanisms to help reduce 
these emissions. The plan recommends specific actions the City can take to meet this target. In 
addition, the Climate Action Plan seeks to analyze the costs and savings associated with proposed 
measures. 
Such a plan would build upon and help implement aspects of the 2015 Ventura County 
Regional Energy Alliance study entitled .. Climate on the Move," which served as a baseline 
assessment of the City's and other Ventura County jurisdictions' GHG emissions and provided 
reduction goals based upon state-issued targets. Such targets are required under California Law (AB 
32). In addition, Senate Bill 3 79 requires local government agencies to include climate adaption and 
resiliency provisions in the safety elements of any general plan revision. 
Deputy Mayor Andrews has an investment interest of $2,000 or more in various oil and gas 
related businesses which include Exxon/Mobil, Spectra Energy2, San Juan Basin Trust, Royal 
Dutch Petroleum, Oneok, Inc, One Gas, Inc. and Duke Energy. 3 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a .. financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. 
Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under 
the Act. The financial interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. {Section 87103{b).) 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
] According to your facts, Spectra Energy merged on February 27, 2017 and became Enbridge, Inc.; which is 
also a New York Stock Exchange listed company. 
J Exxon/Mobil, Oneok, Inc, Royal Dutch Petroleum One Gas, Inc. and Duke Energy Spectra Energy all have 
annual revenues in the billions of dollars, and the San Juan Basin Trust has an annual revenue in the tens of millions of 
dollars. 


File No. A-17-141 
Page No. 3 
aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the 
personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
As Deputy Mayor for the City of San Buenaventura, Mr. Andrews is a public official. 
Deputy Mayor Andrews has an investment interest of $2,000 or more in Exxon/Mobil, Spectra 
Energy, San Juan Basin Trust, Royal Dutch Petroleum, Oneok, Inc, One Gas, Inc. and Duke 
Energy. (Section 87102(a).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality: 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. 
{Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on an interest will be material, depending on the nature of the interest. 
Your facts indicate that none of Deputy Mayor Andrews' investment interests would be 
explicitly involved in decisions on the Climate Action Plan. 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides the materiality test for indirectly involved business entities 
that are sources of income. Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides in pertinent part as follows: 
'"[The] financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information 
would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, 
or the value of a privately-held business." 
All of Deputy Mayor Andrews' investments are in publicly traded stocks and in companies 
listed and traded on the New York Stock Exchange. Given size of the annual revenues of these oil 
and gas companies, the effect of the decision to adopt a Climate Action Plan would not likely 
contribute to a change in the value of any of the companies' publicly traded stock. 
Therefore, we find that the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Deputy Mayor Andrews' financial interests. 


File No. A-17-141 
Page No. 4 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act would not prohibit Deputy Mayor Andrews from 
participating in the decision to adopt a Climate Action Plan. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
3.ry)sL 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

decisions concerning the City's adoption of a Climate Action Plan?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Decisions concerning the Climate Action Plan do not appear to have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on Deputy Mayor Andrews' investment interests.
